---
title: "U.S. DOE H2O Wave Hindcast"
subtitle: "Open-Source Data Resource Characterization Example"
number-sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
# Include every references.bib entry in the reference list, cited or not.
nocite: |
  @*
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
  ipynb: default
  typst:
    papersize: us-letter
    margin:
      x: 0.75in
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
jupyter: python3
---

This document characterizes the wave energy resource at U.S. marine energy test
sites using the U.S. DOE WPTO High-Resolution Wave Hindcast. Data access goes
through the
[`us-marine-energy-resource`](https://pypi.org/project/us-marine-energy-resource/)
package, which finds the hindcast grid node nearest a coordinate and downloads its
record. The analysis covers aggregate statistics, monthly climatology, joint
probability, extreme sea states, exceedance, directionality, and a multi-site
comparison, using [MHKiT](https://mhkit-software.github.io/MHKiT/) for the wave
calculations.

# Setup

Platform bootstrap (Kaggle detection, dependency install, cache seeding, output
paths) lives in `h2o_examples/environment.py`; figure and table helpers live in
`h2o_examples/figures.py`. Importing `environment` first matters: on Kaggle it
installs dependencies before numpy/pandas are imported.

```{python}
import os
import sys
from pathlib import Path

# On Kaggle the h2o_examples helpers ship in the companion dataset; make them
# importable before anything else. Kaggle mounts datasets a few levels deep
# (e.g. /kaggle/input/datasets/<user>/<slug>/), so search recursively rather
# than assuming */h2o_examples.
_pkg = next(Path("/kaggle/input").rglob("h2o_examples/__init__.py"), None)
if _pkg is not None:
    sys.path.insert(0, str(_pkg.parent.parent))
elif os.environ.get("KAGGLE_KERNEL_RUN_TYPE"):
    _listing = sorted(str(p) for p in Path("/kaggle/input").rglob("*"))
    raise RuntimeError(
        "h2o_examples not found under /kaggle/input -- is the "
        "h2o-wave-hindcast-cache dataset attached? /kaggle/input contains:\n"
        + "\n".join(_listing[:80])
    )

from h2o_examples.environment import CACHE_DIR
```

```{python}
import inspect

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns

from mhkit.wave import contours, graphics, performance
from us_marine_energy_resource import wave_hindcast
from us_marine_energy_resource.wave_hindcast.errors import InvalidAttributeError

from h2o_examples.cache import memoize_pickle
from h2o_examples.figures import FIG_DIR, FIG_WIDTH, plot_wave_matrix, savefig, show_table
from h2o_examples.mapping import render_sites_map_outputs
from h2o_examples.sites import WAVE_ENERGY_TEST_SITES, make_sites_map, site_lat_lng
```

# Wave Energy Test Sites {#sec-sites}

The sites live in `h2o_examples/sites.py`, which holds their names, coordinates,
the published PacWave corner provenance, and the folium map helpers. This document
works from a name and a `latitude, longitude` pair.

```{python}
_ = render_sites_map_outputs(WAVE_ENERGY_TEST_SITES, make_sites_map, output_dir=FIG_DIR)
```

Every site as a lat/lon pair (@tbl-site-coordinates). For the two PacWave sites
this is the centerpoint computed from the published corners. The `Domain` column
is resolved from the coordinates by `wave_hindcast.describe_point`, an offline
lookup against the hindcast's grid-node index. It needs no download and no API key.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-site-coordinates
#| tbl-cap: "Wave energy test sites and their hindcast domains"
site_coordinates = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        {
            "Site": name,
            "Label": site["label"],
            "Domain": wave_hindcast.describe_point(*site_lat_lng(site))["domain"],
            "Latitude": site["lat"],
            "Longitude": site["lng"],
        }
        for name, site in WAVE_ENERGY_TEST_SITES.items()
    ]
)
show_table(site_coordinates)
```

# Downloading Data {#sec-downloading}

We read the 3-hour bulk-parameter hindcast [@MHKDR_Dataset_326] through
`wave_hindcast.get_data_at_point(lat, lon, ...)`, which finds the hindcast grid
node nearest a coordinate and returns its record as a DataFrame. One query per
site feeds every deliverable below, `mean_wave_direction` included.

## Backends

The dataset is large behind the scenes, with six domains, 42 years, and roughly
80 GB of `.h5` per domain-year, so query only the years and variables you need.
Two backends serve the data and the package picks between them by request size.
The S3 backend reads the published files anonymously (free, no key, no request
limits, but raw file access that downloads slowly at volume). The NLR API backend
builds an archive server side (free API key required, suited to large pulls).
Every query in this document is small enough to stay on S3, and every fetch is
cached on disk under `~/.mer_wave_cache`, so repeat renders read from disk.

## The same query from the command line

The package installs a CLI. `--info` resolves the nearest grid node offline, with
no download and no key:

```{python}
# The `mer` executable lives next to the Python interpreter running this document.
MER = Path(sys.executable).parent / "mer"
!{MER} wave 44.5670,-124.2290 --info
```

A download looks like this. It is shown rather than run, because the Python cells
below fetch the same data through the API (add `--dry-run` to see the plan without
downloading):

```bash
mer wave 44.5670,-124.2290 --years 2020 \
    --variables significant_wave_height,energy_period,omni-directional_wave_power,mean_wave_direction,peak_period
```

## The Python query

```{python}
SELECTED_SITE = "US_Oregon_PacWave_South"
YEAR = 2020  # the newest published year, served by the S3 backend in every domain
lat_lon = site_lat_lng(WAVE_ENERGY_TEST_SITES[SELECTED_SITE])

# Single source for the site's identity in prose. Quarto renders these inline in
# the narrative below (e.g. `{python} SITE_LABEL`). Figure captions name the site
# literally, because Quarto does not evaluate inline code inside a `fig-cap`; if
# you change SELECTED_SITE, update the single-site captions to match.
SITE_LABEL = WAVE_ENERGY_TEST_SITES[SELECTED_SITE]["label"]
SITE_COORDS = f"{lat_lon[0]:.4f}, {lat_lon[1]:.4f}"
```

```{python}
# The five variables this document uses, in the dataset's canonical snake_case
# spelling, and the short names they carry from here on.
VARIABLES = [
    "significant_wave_height",
    "energy_period",
    "omni-directional_wave_power",
    "mean_wave_direction",
    "peak_period",
]

COLUMN_NAMES = {
    "significant_wave_height": "Hm0",
    "energy_period": "Te",
    "omni-directional_wave_power": "J",
    "mean_wave_direction": "Dir",
    "peak_period": "Tp",
}

# Sentinel written into the data for timesteps the model did not produce.
FILL_VALUE = -9.0
```

```{python}
def file_variable_names(requested, valid):
    """Map canonical dataset names onto one file's own spellings.

    The Gulf domain writes `omnidirectional_wave_power` where every other domain
    hyphenates `omni-directional_wave_power`; matching with the punctuation
    stripped absorbs that drift without a per-domain alias table.
    """
    def slug(name):
        return name.replace("-", "").replace("_", "")

    by_slug = {slug(name): name for name in valid}
    return [by_slug.get(slug(name), name) for name in requested]


def load_site(name, years=(YEAR,)):
    """Hm0/Te/J/Dir/Tp for one site via `wave_hindcast.get_data_at_point`.

    The year span is baked into the cache name so a one-year record and a
    multi-year record of the same site never shadow each other in the package's
    cache. Pass `force=True` through to `get_data_at_point` to re-download.

    Returned as float64, and not as a harmless detail: five of mhkit's ten
    contour methods compute an internal default from the data
    (`max_x1 = x1.max() * 2`) and then reject it with `TypeError: max_x1 must be
    of type float` when handed float32, because `np.float32` is not a Python
    float — `np.float64` is. Casting once, here, keeps every method reachable.
    """
    years = sorted(years)
    span = str(years[0]) if len(years) == 1 else f"{years[0]}-{years[-1]}"
    lat_lon = site_lat_lng(WAVE_ENERGY_TEST_SITES[name])
    query = {"name": f"{name}_{span}", "years": years, "variables": VARIABLES}
    try:
        df = wave_hindcast.get_data_at_point(*lat_lon, **query)
    except InvalidAttributeError as err:  # this file spells a name differently
        query["variables"] = file_variable_names(VARIABLES, err.valid)
        df = wave_hindcast.get_data_at_point(*lat_lon, **query)

    attrs = dict(df.attrs)
    # Columns come back Title Cased in the requested order; use the short names.
    df.columns = list(COLUMN_NAMES.values())
    # Drop rows at the fill sentinel (-9 or -999), see the note below.
    df = df[(df > FILL_VALUE + 0.1).all(axis=1)]
    df["J"] = df["J"] / 1000.0  # W/m -> kW/m
    df = df.astype("float64")
    df.attrs = attrs
    return df


def record_hours(df):
    """Hours the record covers: sea states times the sampling interval."""
    return len(df) * (df.index[1] - df.index[0]).total_seconds() / 3600


data = load_site(SELECTED_SITE)
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Missing data is a fill value, not `NaN`

Timesteps the model never produced are written as an in-range sentinel (`-9` or
`-999`), not `NaN`, so an unmasked read biases every statistic silently.
`load_site` drops any row at or below the sentinel:

```python
df = df[(df > FILL_VALUE + 0.1).all(axis=1)]
```
:::

# Exploring the Downloaded Data {#sec-exploring}

## Metadata

Every fetch carries its metadata on `DataFrame.attrs`: the resolved grid node,
its true position, the units per variable, and the source that served it.
`describe_point` reports the same node resolution offline, plus the served year
span and the node's distance from the requested coordinate.

```{python}
info = wave_hindcast.describe_point(*lat_lon)

metadata = pd.Series(
    {
        "site": WAVE_ENERGY_TEST_SITES[SELECTED_SITE]["label"],
        "domain": data.attrs["domain"],
        "source": data.attrs["source"],
        "requested lat, lon": f"{lat_lon[0]:.4f}, {lat_lon[1]:.4f}",
        "nearest node lat, lon": f"{data.attrs['node_lat']:.4f}, {data.attrs['node_lon']:.4f}",
        "distance to node [m]": round(info["distance_m"], 1),
        "node index (gid)": data.attrs["gid"],
        "years served": f"{info['years'][0]}-{info['years'][1]}",
        "year loaded": YEAR,
        "sea states": len(data),
        "timestep [s]": int((data.index[1] - data.index[0]).total_seconds()),
        **{f"unit, {label}": unit for label, unit in data.attrs["units"].items()},
    },
    name=SELECTED_SITE,
)
# Printed, not returned. Quarto's ipynb writer keeps stream output but drops both
# pandas' HTML table repr (blank cell) and IPython Markdown output (no cell at all),
# so `print(...)` is the only form that survives into the notebook deliverable.
print(metadata.to_string())
```

## Data Preview

```{python}
print(data.head().to_string())
```

# Aggregate Statistics {#sec-aggregate}

Sections 4 through 10 characterize one site, `{python} SITE_LABEL` at
`{python} SITE_COORDS`, read for `{python} str(YEAR)` from the
`{python} data.attrs["source"]` backend of the WPTO hindcast. Each single-site
figure caption repeats the site and coordinates so a figure identifies its source
on its own.

Aggregate wave energy statistics come from mhkit's `performance.statistics`; its
docstring describes the fields.

```{python}
print(inspect.getdoc(performance.statistics))
```

```{python}
print(
    pd.DataFrame(
        {q: performance.statistics(data[q]) for q in ["Hm0", "Te", "J"]}
    ).to_string()
)
```

# Monthly Climatology {#sec-climatology}

When the resource arrives is a device-performance question: the monthly cycle
sets what a device produces season by season. The monthly distributions are in
@fig-monthly-boxplot-hm0 through @fig-monthly-boxplot-j, and the daily structure
in @fig-daily-climatology-hm0 through @fig-daily-climatology-j.

```{python}
import calendar

QOIS = {"Hm0": "$H_{m0}$ [m]", "Te": "$T_e$ [s]", "J": "$J$ [kW/m]"}
months = np.arange(1, 13)
month_labels = [calendar.month_abbr[m] for m in months]  # 'Jan'..'Dec'
colors = sns.color_palette()  # default seaborn color cycle

doy = data.index.dayofyear
dgrp = data.groupby(doy)
month_starts = [pd.Timestamp(YEAR, m, 1).dayofyear for m in months]


def qcolor(q):
    """One fixed color per quantity, shared by every figure in this section."""
    return colors[list(QOIS).index(q)]


def monthly_boxplot(q):
    """Box-and-whisker of the 3-hourly distribution of `q` per month."""
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(FIG_WIDTH, 4))
    sns.boxplot(x=data.index.month, y=data[q], ax=ax, color=qcolor(q))
    ax.set(xlabel="Month", ylabel=QOIS[q])
    ax.set_xticks(range(12))
    ax.set_xticklabels(month_labels)
    fig.tight_layout()
    savefig(f"monthly_boxplot_{q.lower()}")


def daily_climatology(q):
    """Daily median with a 25-75% band and a 7-day average across the year."""
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(FIG_WIDTH, 4))
    stats = dgrp[q].describe()
    days = stats.index
    ax.fill_between(days, stats["25%"], stats["75%"], color=qcolor(q),
                    alpha=0.3, label="Daily 25–75%")
    ax.plot(days, stats["50%"], color=qcolor(q), label="Daily Median")
    roll = stats["50%"].rolling(7, center=True, min_periods=1).mean()
    ax.plot(days, roll, color="0.4", linewidth=2, label="7-day avg")
    ax.set(xlabel="Month", ylabel=QOIS[q])
    ax.margins(x=0)
    ax.set_xticks(month_starts)
    ax.set_xticklabels(month_labels)
    ax.legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1), borderaxespad=0)
    fig.tight_layout()
    savefig(f"daily_climatology_{q.lower()}")


def rolling_median(q, window):
    """`window`-day moving average of the daily median of `q`."""
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(FIG_WIDTH, 3.2))
    roll = dgrp[q].median().rolling(window, center=True, min_periods=1).mean()
    ax.plot(roll.index, roll, color=qcolor(q), label=f"{window}-day avg")
    ax.set(xlabel="Month", ylabel=QOIS[q])
    ax.margins(x=0)
    ax.set_xticks(month_starts)
    ax.set_xticklabels(month_labels)
    fig.tight_layout()
    savefig(f"rolling_{window}d_{q.lower()}")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-monthly-boxplot-hm0
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), monthly distribution of significant wave height ($H_{m0}$)"
monthly_boxplot("Hm0")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-monthly-boxplot-te
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), monthly distribution of energy period ($T_e$)"
monthly_boxplot("Te")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-monthly-boxplot-j
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), monthly distribution of omnidirectional wave energy flux ($J$)"
monthly_boxplot("J")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-daily-climatology-hm0
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), daily significant wave height ($H_{m0}$): median, 25-75% band, and 7-day average"
daily_climatology("Hm0")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-daily-climatology-te
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), daily energy period ($T_e$): median, 25-75% band, and 7-day average"
daily_climatology("Te")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-daily-climatology-j
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), daily omnidirectional wave energy flux ($J$): median, 25-75% band, and 7-day average"
daily_climatology("J")
```

A 7-day average still tracks individual storm systems; a 30-day average smooths
past them and leaves the seasonal signal on its own
(@fig-rolling-7d-j, @fig-rolling-30d-j).

```{python}
#| label: fig-rolling-7d-j
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), 7-day moving average of the daily median omnidirectional wave energy flux ($J$)"
rolling_median("J", 7)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-rolling-30d-j
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), 30-day moving average of the daily median omnidirectional wave energy flux ($J$)"
rolling_median("J", 30)
```

## Monthly bars

One labeled bar per month (@fig-monthly-bar-hm0-mean through
@fig-monthly-bar-j-p95). Mean and 95th percentile are separate figures: the mean is roughly what a device harvests in an average month, the 95th
percentile is the tail it must survive, and paired bars on one axis would let the
tail squash the mean flat.

```{python}
def monthly_bar(q, stat_label, agg, fmt, name):
    """One labeled bar per month of `q`, aggregated by `agg`."""
    monthly = data[q].groupby(data.index.month).agg(agg)
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(FIG_WIDTH, 4))
    bars = ax.bar(months, monthly.reindex(months).values, color=qcolor(q))
    ax.bar_label(bars, fmt=fmt, padding=2, fontsize=9, color="0.25")
    ax.set(xlabel="Month", ylabel=f"{stat_label} {QOIS[q]}")
    ax.set_xticks(months)
    ax.set_xticklabels(month_labels)
    ax.margins(y=0.14)  # headroom so the tallest bar's label is not clipped
    fig.tight_layout()
    savefig(name)


p95 = lambda s: s.quantile(0.95)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-monthly-bar-hm0-mean
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), mean significant wave height ($H_{m0}$) per month"
monthly_bar("Hm0", "Mean", "mean", "%.2f", "monthly_bar_hm0_mean")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-monthly-bar-hm0-p95
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), 95th percentile significant wave height ($H_{m0}$) per month"
monthly_bar("Hm0", "95th percentile", p95, "%.2f", "monthly_bar_hm0_p95")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-monthly-bar-j-mean
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), mean omnidirectional wave energy flux ($J$) per month"
monthly_bar("J", "Mean", "mean", "%.1f", "monthly_bar_j_mean")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-monthly-bar-j-p95
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), 95th percentile omnidirectional wave energy flux ($J$) per month"
monthly_bar("J", "95th percentile", p95, "%.1f", "monthly_bar_j_p95")
```

# Joint Probability Distribution {#sec-jpd}

Joint probability of occurrence (percent of the year) over the ($H_{m0}$, $T_e$)
sea-state bins (@fig-jpd-frequency). This matrix is the resource half of device
performance: paired with a device power matrix it becomes AEP in @sec-aep.

```{python}
Hm0_bins = np.arange(0, np.ceil(data.Hm0.max()) + 0.5, 0.5)
Te_bins = np.arange(0, np.ceil(data.Te.max()) + 1, 1)

# Joint probability of occurrence (reused as the AEP frequency matrix in @sec-aep).
frequency = performance.wave_energy_flux_matrix(
    data.Hm0, data.Te, data.J, "frequency", Hm0_bins, Te_bins
)
print(frequency.to_string())
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-jpd-frequency
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), joint probability of ($H_{m0}$, $T_e$) sea states, percent of the year"
plot_wave_matrix(frequency * 100, zlabel="Joint Probability [% of year]")
savefig("jpd_frequency_matrix")
```

# Extreme Sea States {#sec-extremes}

Fifty- and one-hundred-year environmental contours bound the extreme sea states a
device must survive (@fig-extreme-contours). This document uses the `PCA` method
throughout, following the Wave Energy Converter Design Response Toolbox
[@coe2016_wdrt]: it applies principal component analysis to $(H_{m0}, T_e)$, fits
an inverse Gaussian in the rotated frame, and reads the contour off a circle of
iso-probability using the inverse first-order reliability method (I-FORM)
[@eckert2016_pca_iform].

Two caveats. `environmental_contours` offers ten methods, and the choice moves the
100-year $H_{m0}$ by meters, more than the site or the record length does. The
values below are PCA's answer, not the only answer (the full ten-method comparison
lives in the experiment version of this document). A single hindcast year is
illustrative, not a formal extreme-value estimate. @sec-contour-records quantifies
how much the record length moves the result.

```{python}
#| label: fig-extreme-contours
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), 50- and 100-year environmental contours ($H_{m0}$ vs $T_e$, PCA)"
# Seconds between sea-state samples (3-hour hindcast -> 10800 s).
dt = (data.index[1] - data.index[0]).total_seconds()

copulas50 = contours.environmental_contours(data.Hm0, data.Te, dt, 50, method="PCA")
copulas100 = contours.environmental_contours(data.Hm0, data.Te, dt, 100, method="PCA")

Hm0_contours = [copulas50["PCA_x1"], copulas100["PCA_x1"]]
Te_contours = [copulas50["PCA_x2"], copulas100["PCA_x2"]]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(FIG_WIDTH, 4))
graphics.plot_environmental_contour(
    data.Te.values,
    data.Hm0.values,
    Te_contours,
    Hm0_contours,
    data_label=WAVE_ENERGY_TEST_SITES[SELECTED_SITE]["label"],
    contour_label=["50-year contour", "100-year contour"],
    x_label="Energy Period, $T_e$ [s]",
    y_label="Significant Wave Height, $H_{m0}$ [m]",
    ax=ax,
)
ax.legend(loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("extreme_contours")
```

```{python}
# Peak of each contour: the largest H_m0 and the T_e it occurs at.
for label, cop in (("50-year", copulas50), ("100-year", copulas100)):
    i = cop["PCA_x1"].argmax()
    print(f"{label}: H_m0 max {cop['PCA_x1'].max():.1f} m at T_e {cop['PCA_x2'][i]:.1f} s")
```

# Exceedance Probability {#sec-exceedance}

Exceedance probability is how often the site exceeds a given sea state, the
survivability view of the same record. @fig-exceedance-hm0 is the empirical
survival function $P(H_{m0} > x)$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-exceedance-hm0
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), exceedance probability of significant wave height ($H_{m0}$)"
hm0_sorted = np.sort(data.Hm0.values)
exceedance = 1.0 - np.arange(1, hm0_sorted.size + 1) / (hm0_sorted.size + 1)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))
ax.semilogy(hm0_sorted, exceedance)
for pct in (0.99, 1.0):
    val = np.quantile(data.Hm0, pct) if pct < 1.0 else data.Hm0.max()
    ax.axvline(val, ls="--", color="0.5")
    ax.text(val, 1e-3, f"{'max' if pct == 1.0 else '99%'}\n{val:.1f} m", ha="center")
ax.set(xlabel="$H_{m0}$ [m]", ylabel="$P(H_{m0} > x)$")
ax.grid(True, which="both", alpha=0.3)
savefig("exceedance_hm0")
```

# Directionality {#sec-directionality}

Where the waves come from decides device orientation and array layout. The wave
rose (@fig-wave-rose) shows how often each direction sector occurs, stacked by
$H_{m0}$; the polar joint probability panels
(@fig-polar-jpd-hm0, @fig-polar-jpd-te, @fig-polar-jpd-j) resolve the same
directions against each variable; and @fig-directionality-mean-power shows where
the mean power arrives from.

::: {.callout-note}
## Direction conventions

Directions come back in the meteorological convention (degrees clockwise from
north, the direction waves arrive *from*) for every domain. The published S3
files already use it, and the package applies the one correction that exists
(Hawaii's API CSVs) automatically, with `describe_point()["direction_transform"]`
recording whether it did. An earlier version of this example inherited a $-90°$
rotation written against a different product (the 1-hour virtual buoy); it is not
applied here, and the raw 3-hour directions at PacWave South are consistent with
the WNW prevailing direction in the site's published specification. Anyone
depending on absolute direction should confirm the convention against a
co-located measured buoy.
:::

```{python}
Dir_bins = np.arange(0, 360 + 15, 15)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-wave-rose
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), wave rose: percent of the year by direction, stacked by significant wave height ($H_{m0}$) band"
rose_bands = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, np.inf]  # Hm0 [m]
band_labels = ["0-1 m", "1-2 m", "2-3 m", "3-4 m", ">4 m"]

rose_sector = pd.cut(data.Dir, Dir_bins, right=False)
band = pd.cut(data.Hm0, rose_bands, labels=band_labels, right=False)
# reindex both ways: crosstab drops empty sectors and empty bands, but the
# stacked bars need all 24 directions and every band.
rose = (
    pd.crosstab(rose_sector, band)
    .reindex(index=rose_sector.cat.categories, columns=band_labels, fill_value=0)
    / len(data) * 100  # % of year
)

theta = np.deg2rad(Dir_bins[:-1] + 7.5)
band_colors = sns.color_palette("viridis", n_colors=len(band_labels))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 7), subplot_kw={"projection": "polar"})
bottom = np.zeros(len(theta))
for label, color in zip(band_labels, band_colors):
    values = rose[label].to_numpy()
    ax.bar(theta, values, width=np.deg2rad(15), bottom=bottom,
           color=color, edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.4, label=label)
    bottom += values
ax.set_theta_zero_location("N")
ax.set_theta_direction(-1)
ax.legend(title="$H_{m0}$", loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.08, 1.0))
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("directionality_wave_rose")
```

```{python}
def polar_jpd(radius, r_bins, r_label, name):
    """Polar joint probability of direction against `radius`, % of year."""
    counts, _, _ = np.histogram2d(data.Dir, radius, bins=[Dir_bins, r_bins])
    counts = counts / counts.sum() * 100  # % of year per (direction, radius) bin
    th, rr = np.meshgrid(np.deg2rad(Dir_bins), r_bins, indexing="ij")
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 6), subplot_kw={"projection": "polar"})
    pcm = ax.pcolormesh(th, rr, counts, shading="auto", cmap="viridis")
    ax.set_theta_zero_location("N")
    ax.set_theta_direction(-1)
    plt.colorbar(pcm, ax=ax, label="% of year", pad=0.1)
    fig.tight_layout()
    savefig(name)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-polar-jpd-hm0
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), joint probability of direction and significant wave height ($H_{m0}$)"
polar_jpd(data.Hm0, Hm0_bins, QOIS["Hm0"], "polar_jpd_hm0")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-polar-jpd-te
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), joint probability of direction and energy period ($T_e$)"
polar_jpd(data.Te, Te_bins, QOIS["Te"], "polar_jpd_te")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-polar-jpd-j
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), joint probability of direction and omnidirectional wave energy flux ($J$)"
polar_jpd(data.J, np.linspace(0, data.J.max(), len(Hm0_bins)), QOIS["J"], "polar_jpd_j")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-directionality-mean-power
#| fig-cap: "PacWave South (44.5670, -124.2290), mean omnidirectional wave energy flux ($J$) by direction sector"
mean_sector = pd.cut(data.Dir, Dir_bins)
mean_J = data.J.groupby(mean_sector, observed=False).mean()
centers = np.deg2rad(Dir_bins[:-1] + 7.5)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6), subplot_kw={"projection": "polar"})
ax.bar(centers, mean_J.fillna(0).values, width=np.deg2rad(15), alpha=0.7)
ax.set_theta_zero_location("N")
ax.set_theta_direction(-1)
savefig("directionality_mean_power")
```

# Annual Energy Production (AEP) {#sec-aep}

AEP is the electrical energy a device would deliver at this site over a year.
Computing it needs two matrices on the same sea-state bins: the resource side,
which @sec-jpd's joint probability matrix already provides, and the device side,
a power matrix, which is a property of a specific WEC and is not carried in
this document.

To run this section, supply a power matrix in exactly this format:

- a pandas DataFrame with the same index and columns as the `frequency` matrix
  from @sec-jpd: rows are the $H_{m0}$ bin centers of `Hm0_bins` (0.5 m steps),
  columns are the $T_e$ bin centers of `Te_bins` (1 s steps)
- each value is the mean electrical power in kW the device delivers in that
  ($H_{m0}$, $T_e$) sea state
- bins the device cannot operate in are 0 (cut-out above, below cut-in), not NaN

AEP is the element-wise product of the two matrices, summed, times the hours in
the record: each bin contributes (fraction of the year) x (power in that bin).
When working from measured device data instead, mhkit's
`performance.mean_annual_energy_production_matrix(CWM, JM, frequency)` computes
the same quantity from a capture width matrix.

```{python}
# POWER_MATRIX: device power [kW] on the same bins as `frequency` (see the format
# above). For example: pd.read_csv("device_power_matrix.csv", index_col=0) with
# the bin centers as index/column labels.
POWER_MATRIX = None

hours_in_record = record_hours(data)

if POWER_MATRIX is not None:
    aep_kwh = float((frequency * POWER_MATRIX).to_numpy().sum()) * hours_in_record
    print(f"AEP: {aep_kwh / 1e3:,.0f} MWh over {hours_in_record:.0f} h")
else:
    print("No power matrix supplied -- section stubbed. See the format above.")
```

# Multiple Sites: Comparison {#sec-multisite}

The single-site pipeline above characterizes one location; here we place several
sites side by side. Edit `SITES_TO_COMPARE`, a list of
`WAVE_ENERGY_TEST_SITES` keys, to add or omit sites. Every fetch lands in the
package cache, so re-runs skip S3 entirely. Every site keeps one color across
every figure below.

The Alaska and Puerto Rico points are open water with no test facility, included
to exercise those domains. The Atlantic domain is omitted for now: multi-year S3
reads of its files (the largest in the dataset) currently stall mid-download.
When re-enabled, Pioneer WEC is the site to prefer over Jennette's Pier. The
pier's narrow, depth-limited $H_{m0}$ range breaks `environmental_contours`
under MHKiT's default binning (`LinAlgError`), a statement about the bin
defaults rather than the ocean, and `bin_val_size=0.10` fixes it.

```{python}
SITES_TO_COMPARE = [
    "US_Oregon_PacWave_South",                 # West_Coast
    "US_Oregon_PacWave_North",                 # West_Coast
    "US_California_Humboldt_Bay",              # West_Coast
    "US_California_San_Luis_Obispo",           # West_Coast
    "US_Hawaii_Oahu_WETS",                     # Hawaii
    "US_Alaska_Kodiak",                        # Alaska
    "US_PuertoRico_North_Shore",               # Gulf_of_Mexico_and_Puerto_Rico
    # "US_Massachusetts_WoodsHole_PioneerWec", # Atlantic; multi-year S3 reads
    #                                          # currently stall -- see above
    # "US_North_Carolina_Jenettes_Pier",       # Atlantic; needs bin_val_size=0.10
    #                                          # to contour -- see the note above
]  # comment a key in/out to include/omit that site

site_data = {}
for name in SITES_TO_COMPARE:
    try:
        site_data[name] = load_site(name)
    except Exception as e:  # e.g. a site outside its domain's grid
        print(f"skipping {name}: {type(e).__name__}: {e}")

# One color per site, shared across every figure below.
tab10 = plt.get_cmap("tab10")
site_colors = {name: tab10(i) for i, name in enumerate(site_data)}

{name: df.shape for name, df in site_data.items()}
```

## Site KPIs

One row per site (@tbl-site-kpis), ranked by annual mean $J$. The annual energy
column is the resource per meter of wave front, mean $J$ integrated over the
record. @fig-mean-vs-p95 sets the mean against the 95th percentile on one linear
axis; the gap between them separates what a device earns from (the mean) and what
it must survive (the tail).

```{python}
#| label: tbl-site-kpis
#| tbl-cap: "Site KPIs for the loaded year"
# Sites ranked by annual mean J, richest first. Every figure below reuses this
# order so the ranking is the same wherever you look.
site_order = sorted(site_data, key=lambda n: site_data[n]["J"].mean(), reverse=True)

kpis = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        WAVE_ENERGY_TEST_SITES[n]["label"]: {
            "mean Hm0 [m]": site_data[n].Hm0.mean(),
            "max Hm0 [m]": site_data[n].Hm0.max(),
            "mean Te [s]": site_data[n].Te.mean(),
            "mean J [kW/m]": site_data[n].J.mean(),
            "p95 J [kW/m]": site_data[n].J.quantile(0.95),
            "annual energy [MWh/m]": site_data[n].J.mean() * record_hours(site_data[n]) / 1000,
        }
        for n in site_order
    }
).T
show_table(kpis.round(1))
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-mean-vs-p95
#| fig-cap: "Annual mean against 95th percentile omnidirectional wave energy flux ($J$) per site"
annual_mean = pd.Series({n: site_data[n]["J"].mean() for n in site_order})
annual_p95 = pd.Series({n: site_data[n]["J"].quantile(0.95) for n in site_order})
rank = annual_mean.sort_values().index  # ascending: richest at the top of the axis

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(FIG_WIDTH, 4.5))
y = np.arange(len(rank))
ax.hlines(y, annual_mean[rank], annual_p95[rank], color="0.75", linewidth=2, zorder=1)
ax.scatter(annual_mean[rank], y, s=70, zorder=2,
           color=[site_colors[n] for n in rank], label="annual mean")
ax.scatter(annual_p95[rank], y, s=70, marker="D", facecolors="none", zorder=2,
           edgecolors=[site_colors[n] for n in rank], label="95th percentile")
for i, n in enumerate(rank):
    for value in (annual_mean[n], annual_p95[n]):
        ax.annotate(f"{value:.0f}", (value, i), xytext=(0, 8),
                    textcoords="offset points", ha="center", size=7, color="0.25")
ax.set_yticks(y)
ax.set_yticklabels([WAVE_ENERGY_TEST_SITES[n]["label"] for n in rank])
ax.set_xlabel("$J$ [kW/m]")
ax.margins(x=0.08, y=0.09)
ax.legend(loc="lower right")
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("multisite_mean_vs_p95")
```

## Moving averages

A centered moving average smooths storm-scale noise and exposes each site's
seasonal cycle. The 7-day window (@fig-multisite-7d-hm0 through
@fig-multisite-7d-j) still tracks individual storms; the 30-day window
(@fig-multisite-30d-hm0 through @fig-multisite-30d-j) leaves the seasonal signal.
Seasonality follows the storm tracks a site is exposed to, not its latitude.

```{python}
def multisite_rolling(q, window, name):
    """`window` centered moving average of `q`, one line per site."""
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(FIG_WIDTH, 4))
    for site, df in site_data.items():
        roll = df[q].rolling(window, center=True, min_periods=1).mean()
        ax.plot(roll.index, roll, color=site_colors[site],
                label=WAVE_ENERGY_TEST_SITES[site]["label"], linewidth=1.5)
    ax.set(xlabel="Date", ylabel=QOIS[q])
    ax.margins(x=0)
    ax.legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1), borderaxespad=0)
    fig.autofmt_xdate()  # rotate/thin the date ticks; unrotated they overlap
    fig.tight_layout()
    savefig(name)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-multisite-7d-hm0
#| fig-cap: "7-day moving average of significant wave height ($H_{m0}$), all sites"
multisite_rolling("Hm0", "7D", "multisite_7d_hm0")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-multisite-7d-te
#| fig-cap: "7-day moving average of energy period ($T_e$), all sites"
multisite_rolling("Te", "7D", "multisite_7d_te")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-multisite-7d-j
#| fig-cap: "7-day moving average of omnidirectional wave energy flux ($J$), all sites"
multisite_rolling("J", "7D", "multisite_7d_j")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-multisite-30d-hm0
#| fig-cap: "30-day moving average of significant wave height ($H_{m0}$), all sites"
multisite_rolling("Hm0", "30D", "multisite_30d_hm0")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-multisite-30d-te
#| fig-cap: "30-day moving average of energy period ($T_e$), all sites"
multisite_rolling("Te", "30D", "multisite_30d_te")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-multisite-30d-j
#| fig-cap: "30-day moving average of omnidirectional wave energy flux ($J$), all sites"
multisite_rolling("J", "30D", "multisite_30d_j")
```

# Environmental Contours: 1, 3 and 5 Year Records {#sec-contour-records}

A 100-year contour extrapolated from one year of data asks a few thousand
3-hour sea states to speak for a century. The size of that error is measurable.
Refit the contour on more years and look at what moves. Here the 100-year PCA
contour is refit per site on 1, 3 and 5 years of record.

`load_site` takes a list of years, so a longer record is one query: the package
fetches the span in a single cached pull (five years times five variables still
sits under the S3/API seam, so it stays on the free backend). Change
`CONTOUR_YEARS` to widen or narrow the window.

```{python}
CONTOUR_YEARS = [2016, 2017, 2018, 2019, 2020]

# Nested windows, all ending at YEAR: 1 -> [2020], 3 -> [2018..2020],
# 5 -> [2016..2020]. Each is a superset of the one before, so any change between
# them comes from added data and not from swapping which years are in play.
YEAR_WINDOWS = {n: CONTOUR_YEARS[-n:] for n in (1, 3, 5)}


def load_site_years(name, years=CONTOUR_YEARS):
    """One site's sea states over a window of years, sliced from the full record.

    The full CONTOUR_YEARS record is fetched (and cached) once per site; every
    window is a view of it, so widening the analysis costs no extra downloads.
    """
    df = load_site(name, years=CONTOUR_YEARS)
    return df[df.index.year.isin(years)]


# Fitting a contour is the expensive step in this document; each fit is a pure
# function of (site, years, method, return_period), so results are memoised to
# .cache/contours/. Set FORCE_CONTOURS = True to recompute everything.
FORCE_CONTOURS = False
CONTOUR_CACHE_DIR = CACHE_DIR / "contours"


def contour(name, years, method="PCA", return_period=100, force=None):
    """100-year (default) contour for one site+record, memoised to disk.

    Returns a dict with 'x1' (Hm0) and 'x2' (Te), or both None if the fit does
    not converge.
    """
    def fit():
        df = load_site_years(name, years=years)
        dt = (df.index[1] - df.index[0]).total_seconds()
        try:
            cop = contours.environmental_contours(
                df.Hm0, df.Te, dt, return_period, method=method)
            return {"x2": np.asarray(cop[f"{method}_x2"]),
                    "x1": np.asarray(cop[f"{method}_x1"])}
        except (np.linalg.LinAlgError, ValueError):
            return {"x2": None, "x1": None}

    key = f"{name}__{years[0]}-{years[-1]}__{method}__{return_period}yr.pkl"
    return memoize_pickle(
        CONTOUR_CACHE_DIR / key, fit,
        force=FORCE_CONTOURS if force is None else force,
    )


def contour_peak(name, years, method="PCA", return_period=100):
    """Peak Hm0 of a cached contour, or NaN if it did not converge."""
    hm0 = contour(name, years, method, return_period)["x1"]
    return np.nan if hm0 is None else float(np.nanmax(hm0))


record = pd.Series(
    {WAVE_ENERGY_TEST_SITES[n]["label"]: len(load_site_years(n)) for n in site_order},
    name=f"sea states, {CONTOUR_YEARS[0]}–{CONTOUR_YEARS[-1]}",
)
print(record.to_string())
```

## The extreme against the length of the record

For each site, the peak of the 100-year contour, the one number a designer takes
away, is recomputed on 1, 3 and 5 years, each window a superset of the last
(@fig-contour-peak-sweep). If a longer record simply refined the estimate, these
lines would settle. They do not: within one to five years the estimate has not
converged, so any single window is one draw from a distribution of answers, and a
number quoted from one window without saying which window is not meaningful.

```{python}
#| label: fig-contour-peak-sweep
#| fig-cap: "Peak 100-year significant wave height ($H_{m0}$) against record length, all sites"
sweep = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        WAVE_ENERGY_TEST_SITES[name]["label"]: {
            n: contour_peak(name, years) for n, years in YEAR_WINDOWS.items()
        }
        for name in site_order
    }
)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(FIG_WIDTH, 5.5))
for name in site_order:
    label = WAVE_ENERGY_TEST_SITES[name]["label"]
    ax.plot(sweep.index, sweep[label], marker="o", color=site_colors[name],
            linewidth=2, label=label)
ax.set(xlabel=f"Years of record (each window ends at {CONTOUR_YEARS[-1]})",
       ylabel="Peak $H_{m0}$ of the 100-year contour [m]")
ax.set_xticks(list(YEAR_WINDOWS))
ax.legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1), borderaxespad=0)
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("contour_peak_vs_record_length")
```

```{python}
table = sweep.T
table.columns = [f"{n} yr" for n in table.columns]
table["range [m]"] = (table.max(axis=1) - table.min(axis=1))
print(table.round(2).to_string())
```

The `range` column is the spread of answers a site gives depending only on how
many years were used, a choice that has nothing to do with the ocean.

## The contour at each record length

The same result as curves (@fig-contour-1yr, @fig-contour-3yr, @fig-contour-5yr):
every site's 100-year contour on one window per figure, on shared axes limits, so
following one color across the figures shows the shape moving, not just its peak.

```{python}
# Fit (or read back) every window's contours once, and frame all three figures
# to the same limits so they compare directly.
window_contours = {
    n: {name: contour(name, years) for name in site_order}
    for n, years in YEAR_WINDOWS.items()
}
_fitted = [c for by_site in window_contours.values()
           for c in by_site.values() if c["x1"] is not None]
te_max = max(np.nanmax(c["x2"]) for c in _fitted)
hm0_max = max(np.nanmax(c["x1"]) for c in _fitted)


def plot_window_contours(n):
    """All sites' 100-year contours for one record-length window."""
    years = YEAR_WINDOWS[n]
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(FIG_WIDTH, 5))
    for name in site_order:
        cop = window_contours[n][name]
        if cop["x1"] is None:
            continue
        ax.plot(cop["x2"], cop["x1"], color=site_colors[name], linewidth=1.8,
                label=WAVE_ENERGY_TEST_SITES[name]["label"])
    ax.set(xlim=(0, te_max * 1.05), ylim=(0, hm0_max * 1.08),
           xlabel="Energy Period, $T_e$ [s]",
           ylabel="Significant Wave Height, $H_{m0}$ [m]")
    ax.legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1), borderaxespad=0,
              fontsize=8)
    fig.tight_layout()
    savefig(f"contour_100yr_{n}yr")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-contour-1yr
#| fig-cap: "100-year contours ($H_{m0}$ vs $T_e$) from a 1-year record (2020), all sites"
plot_window_contours(1)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-contour-3yr
#| fig-cap: "100-year contours ($H_{m0}$ vs $T_e$) from a 3-year record (2018-2020), all sites"
plot_window_contours(3)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-contour-5yr
#| fig-cap: "100-year contours ($H_{m0}$ vs $T_e$) from a 5-year record (2016-2020), all sites"
plot_window_contours(5)
```

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
